title: Quiz: Single Photons and Polarization Encoding
description: Test your understanding of photonic qubits, polarization states, and encoding schemes

questions.question: In polarization encoding for photonic qubits, what states typically form the computational basis?

options.text: Right circular (R) and Left circular (L) polarizations
Any orthogonal pair of polarization states

questions.explanation: The standard computational basis for polarization qubits uses Horizontal (H) for |0> and Vertical (V) for |1>. While any orthogonal pair could be used in principle, H/V is the conventional choice.

questions.question: The diagonal polarization state |D> can be expressed as:
questions.explanation: The diagonal polarization |D> is an equal superposition of H and V with zero relative phase: |D> = (|H> + |V>)/sqrt(2). This corresponds to the |+> state on the Bloch sphere.

questions.question: What is dual-rail (path) encoding for photonic qubits?

options.text: Encoding information in two different wavelengths
Using two photons to encode one qubit
Encoding a qubit in which spatial path a single photon occupies
Using both polarization and time-bin encoding simultaneously

questions.explanation: In dual-rail (path) encoding, a single photon encodes a qubit based on which of two spatial modes it occupies. |0> means the photon is in the upper path, |1> means it's in the lower path.

questions.question: Why is time-bin encoding preferred for long-distance fiber quantum communication?

options.text: It is immune to polarization mode dispersion and drift in fibers
It has higher single-photon detection efficiency

questions.explanation: Time-bin encoding is stable because the early/late time bins experience nearly identical conditions as they travel through the fiber. Polarization, in contrast, can rotate unpredictably in long fibers.

questions.question: In a Mach-Zehnder interferometer with phase difference phi, what is the probability of detecting a single photon at detector 1?
questions.explanation: For a balanced Mach-Zehnder interferometer, the detection probability at detector 1 is cos^2(phi/2) and at detector 2 is sin^2(phi/2), showing interference fringes as the phase varies.

questions.question: Photonic qubits require cryogenic cooling to maintain quantum coherence.
questions.explanation: False! This is one of the major advantages of photonic qubits. Photons interact very weakly with the environment and can maintain coherence at room temperature. The detectors may need cooling (especially SNSPDs), but the qubits themselves do not.

questions.question: What is the Jones vector representation of right circular polarization |R>?
questions.explanation: Right circular polarization has Jones vector (1, i)^T / sqrt(2), representing an equal superposition of H and V with a 90-degree phase difference. Left circular uses (1, -i)^T / sqrt(2).

questions.question: What is the fundamental challenge that makes photonic quantum computing difficult?

options.text: Photons travel too fast to control
Photons have very weak photon-photon interactions
Photons always decohere in optical fibers

questions.explanation: Photons do not naturally interact with each other, which makes implementing two-qubit gates extremely challenging. This is overcome in the KLM scheme using measurement-induced nonlinearity, but the gates become probabilistic.
